Note

License reminder: this project is distributed under the MIT License. See the repository LICENSE file. Disclaimer: this simulator is provided as-is for research, calibration, and educational use. Users assume all risks, including any direct or indirect damage, data loss, hardware damage, injury, or regulatory non-compliance arising from use or misuse.

Core Module

src.core.motor_model

BLDC Motor Model

src.core.power_model

Power Supply Model

src.core.simulation_engine

Simulation Engine Module

src.core.load_model

Load Model Module

BLDC Motor Model

This module implements the mathematical model of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor, including electrical and mechanical dynamics equations. The model accepts 3-phase voltages and produces motor variables (currents, EMF, torque, speed, position).

The electrical model is based on: - Phase voltage equation: v_phase = R*i_phase + L*di_phase/dt + e_back_phase - Back-EMF generation from rotor position

The mechanical model includes: - Newton’s second law: tau_electro - tau_load - f*omega = J*d(omega)/dt - Position integration: d(theta)/dt = omega

author:

BLDC Control Team

version:

0.8.0

Added in version 0.8.0: Initial implementation of BLDC motor model

class src.core.motor_model.MotorParameters(model_type='scalar', emf_shape='trapezoidal', nominal_voltage=48.0, phase_resistance=2.5, phase_inductance=0.005, back_emf_constant=0.1, torque_constant=0.1, rotor_inertia=0.0005, friction_coefficient=0.001, num_poles=8, poles_pairs=4, ld=None, lq=None, flux_weakening_id_coefficient=0.0, flux_weakening_min_ratio=0.2)[source]

Bases: object

Data class containing BLDC motor parameters.

Variables:

  • model_type – Type of motor model (‘scalar’ or ‘dq’)

  • nominal_voltage – Nominal DC voltage [V]

  • phase_resistance – Phase winding resistance [Ohms]

  • phase_inductance – Phase winding inductance [H]

  • back_emf_constant – Back-EMF constant [V*s/rad]

  • torque_constant – Electromagnetic torque constant [N*m/A]

  • rotor_inertia – Rotor moment of inertia [kg*m^2]

  • friction_coefficient – Viscous friction coefficient [N*m*s/rad]

  • num_poles – Number of magnetic poles

  • poles_pairs – Number of pole pairs (num_poles/2)

model_type: str = 'scalar'
emf_shape: str = 'trapezoidal'
nominal_voltage: float = 48.0
phase_resistance: float = 2.5
phase_inductance: float = 0.005
back_emf_constant: float = 0.1
torque_constant: float = 0.1
rotor_inertia: float = 0.0005
friction_coefficient: float = 0.001
num_poles: int = 8
poles_pairs: int = 4
ld: float | None = None
lq: float | None = None
flux_weakening_id_coefficient: float = 0.0
flux_weakening_min_ratio: float = 0.2
__init__(model_type='scalar', emf_shape='trapezoidal', nominal_voltage=48.0, phase_resistance=2.5, phase_inductance=0.005, back_emf_constant=0.1, torque_constant=0.1, rotor_inertia=0.0005, friction_coefficient=0.001, num_poles=8, poles_pairs=4, ld=None, lq=None, flux_weakening_id_coefficient=0.0, flux_weakening_min_ratio=0.2)
class src.core.motor_model.BLDCMotor(parameters, dt=0.0001)[source]

Bases: object

BLDC Motor Model

Implements a 3-phase BLDC motor model with electrical and mechanical dynamics. Uses numerical integration (4th-order Runge-Kutta) for state evolution.

The motor model tracks: - Three phase currents: i_a, i_b, i_c [A] - Rotor angular velocity: omega [rad/s] - Rotor angular position: theta [rad] - Phase voltages (input): v_a, v_b, v_c [V]

State Vector: [i_a, i_b, i_c, omega, theta]

Example:
>>> params = MotorParameters(
...     nominal_voltage=48.0,
...     phase_resistance=2.5,
...     phase_inductance=0.005,
...     back_emf_constant=0.1,
...     torque_constant=0.1,
...     rotor_inertia=0.0005,
...     friction_coefficient=0.001,
...     num_poles=8,
...     poles_pairs=4
... )
>>> motor = BLDCMotor(params, dt=0.0001)
>>> voltages = np.array([10.0, -5.0, -5.0])
>>> motor.step(voltages, load_torque=0.5)
__init__(parameters, dt=0.0001)[source]

Initialize the BLDC motor model.

Parameters:

  • parameters (MotorParameters) – Motor parameters (MotorParameters)

  • dt (float) – Simulation time step [seconds], defaults to 0.0001

Raises:

  • ValueError – If dt <= 0

  • ValueError – If phase_inductance <= 0

property currents: ndarray

Get phase currents [A].

Returns:

Array of [i_a, i_b, i_c]

Return type:

np.ndarray

property omega: float

Get rotor angular velocity [rad/s].

Returns:

Angular velocity

Return type:

float

property theta: float

Get rotor angular position [rad].

Returns:

Angular position (wrapped to [0, 2*pi])

Return type:

float

property speed_rpm: float

Get rotor speed in revolutions per minute [RPM].

Returns:

Speed in RPM

Return type:

float

property back_emf: ndarray

Get back-EMF for each phase [V].

Returns:

Array of [e_a, e_b, e_c]

Return type:

np.ndarray

property electromagnetic_torque: float

Get electromagnetic torque [N*m].

Returns:

Torque

Return type:

float

step(voltages, load_torque=0.0)[source]

Execute one simulation step using 4th-order Runge-Kutta integration.

Updates internal state: [i_a, i_b, i_c, omega, theta]

Runge-Kutta 4th Order (RK4):

\[k_1 = f(t_n, y_n)\]
\[k_2 = f(t_n + \frac{h}{2}, y_n + \frac{h}{2}k_1)\]
\[k_3 = f(t_n + \frac{h}{2}, y_n + \frac{h}{2}k_2)\]
\[k_4 = f(t_n + h, y_n + h*k_3)\]
\[y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)\]
Parameters:

  • voltages (ndarray) – Applied phase voltages [v_a, v_b, v_c] [V]

  • load_torque (float) – Load torque acting on motor shaft [N*m], defaults to 0.0

Return type:

None

Warning

Voltages must be 3-element array. Use SVM generator for modulated voltages.

reset(initial_speed=0.0)[source]

Reset motor to initial conditions.

Parameters:

initial_speed (float) – Initial rotor speed [rad/s], defaults to 0.0

Return type:

None

get_state_dict()[source]

Get all motor state variables as a dictionary.

Returns:

Dictionary containing all motor variables

Return type:

dict

Keys:

  • ‘currents_a’: Phase A current [A]

  • ‘currents_b’: Phase B current [A]

  • ‘currents_c’: Phase C current [A]

  • ‘omega’: Angular velocity [rad/s]

  • ‘theta’: Angular position [rad]

  • ‘speed_rpm’: Speed [RPM]

  • ‘back_emf_a’: Phase A back-EMF [V]

  • ‘back_emf_b’: Phase B back-EMF [V]

  • ‘back_emf_c’: Phase C back-EMF [V]

  • ‘torque’: Electromagnetic torque [N*m]

Power Supply Model

This module provides models of the DC power supply feeding the inverter. A time-dependent voltage profile can be defined to simulate battery sag, AC ripple rectification, or other variations.

author:

BLDC Control Team

version:

0.8.0

Added in version 0.8.0: Initial implementation of power supply models

src.core.power_model.compute_power_metrics(voltage, current, backend='cpu')[source]

Compute basic power-quality metrics from voltage/current waveforms.

Return type:

dict[str, float]

Parameters

voltagenp.ndarray

Instantaneous voltage samples.

currentnp.ndarray

Instantaneous current samples, aligned with voltage.

Returns

dict

Active/apparent/reactive power, RMS values, and power factor.

src.core.power_model.required_reactive_compensation(active_power_w, current_pf, target_pf)[source]

Estimate reactive compensation needed to improve power factor.

Return type:

dict[str, float]

Notes

This helper assumes positive active power and lagging-load correction sizing.

src.core.power_model.compute_efficiency_metrics(input_power_w, torque_nm, omega_rad_s)[source]

Compute simple drivetrain efficiency metrics from power flow values.

Return type:

dict[str, float]

src.core.power_model.recommend_efficiency_adjustments(efficiency, power_factor, device_drop_v=0.0, dead_time_fraction=0.0, conduction_resistance_ohm=0.0, switching_frequency_hz=0.0, switching_loss_coeff_v_per_a_khz=0.0, target_efficiency=0.9)[source]

Return simple heuristic suggestions for improving simulated efficiency.

Return type:

dict[str, object]

class src.core.power_model.PowerFactorController(target_pf=0.95, kp=0.1, ki=1.0, max_compensation_var=10000.0)[source]

Bases: object

Simple closed-loop power factor correction command generator.

The controller estimates a reactive compensation command from current PF, active power, and a target PF. It is designed as a non-invasive telemetry hook for simulation workflows.

__init__(target_pf=0.95, kp=0.1, ki=1.0, max_compensation_var=10000.0)[source]
reset()[source]
Return type:

None

update(current_pf, active_power_w, dt)[source]

Update controller and return recommended compensation [VAR].

Return type:

float

Bases: ABC

Abstract base class for supply voltage profiles.

abstractmethod get_voltage(time)[source]

Return supply voltage at time t.

Return type:

float

reset()[source]

Reset profile state if applicable.

Return type:

None

Constant DC voltage supply.

__init__(voltage=48.0)[source]
get_voltage(time)[source]

Return supply voltage at time t.

Return type:

float

Bases: SupplyProfile

Linear ramp of supply voltage.

Allows simulating startup charge or sag.

__init__(initial, final, duration)[source]
get_voltage(time)[source]

Return supply voltage at time t.

Return type:

Custom time-dependent supply using function or data points.

get_voltage(time)[source]

Return supply voltage at time t.

Return type:

float

Simulation Engine Module

This module implements the main simulation loop that coordinates motor model, control blocks, load profile, and data logging.

Provides efficient real-time simulation with optimized calculations.

author:

BLDC Control Team

version:

0.8.0

Added in version 0.8.0: Initial implementation of simulation engine

class src.core.simulation_engine.SimulationEngine(motor, load_profile, dt=0.0001, max_history=100000, supply_profile=None, pfc_controller=None, pfc_window_samples=128, hardware_interface=None, current_sense=None, compute_backend='auto')[source]

Bases: object

Main simulation engine for BLDC motor control.

Orchestrates motor simulation with: - Motor model state evolution - Load profile application - Voltage input (from SVM or control blocks) - Data logging and history tracking

Features: - Efficient NumPy-based calculations - Real-time data logging - State history tracking - Simulation time management

Example:
>>> params = MotorParameters(...)
>>> motor = BLDCMotor(params, dt=0.0001)
>>> load = ConstantLoad(torque=1.0)
>>> engine = SimulationEngine(motor, load, dt=0.0001)
>>>
>>> for i in range(10000):
...     voltages = np.array([10, -5, -5])
...     engine.step(voltages)
>>>
>>> history = engine.get_history()
__init__(motor, load_profile, dt=0.0001, max_history=100000, supply_profile=None, pfc_controller=None, pfc_window_samples=128, hardware_interface=None, current_sense=None, compute_backend='auto')[source]

Initialize simulation engine.

Parameters:

  • motor (BLDCMotor) – BLDC motor model (BLDCMotor)

  • load_profile (LoadProfile) – Load profile (LoadProfile)

  • dt (float) – Simulation time step [seconds], defaults to 0.0001

  • max_history (int) – Maximum history samples to store, defaults to 100000

Raises:

ValueError – If dt <= 0

set_inverter_telemetry(telemetry)[source]

Update latest inverter telemetry packet from the modulation stage.

Return type:

None

get_inverter_state()[source]

Return latest inverter telemetry and runtime diagnostics.

Return type:

dict[str, float | int | bool]

set_pwm_frequency(pwm_frequency_hz)[source]

Configure PWM/control period metadata used for timing telemetry.

Return type:

None

record_control_timing(calc_duration_s, control_period_s=None)[source]

Record controller compute duration and update CPU-load estimates.

Return type:

None

get_control_timing_state()[source]

Return runtime control-timing and CPU-load telemetry.

Return type:

dict[str, float]

get_numerical_stability_advisory()[source]

Return RK4 dt-stability guidance based on current motor parameters and dt.

Guidance intentionally recommends adjusting only simulation settings: reducing dt and/or increasing PWM/switching frequency.

Return type:

dict[str, float | str | bool]

set_compute_backend(backend)[source]

Set compute backend policy: auto, cpu, or gpu.

Return type:

None

get_compute_backend_state()[source]

Return currently selected compute backend state.

Return type:

dict[str, Any]

step(voltages, log_data=True)[source]

Execute one simulation step.

Updates motor state, applies load, and optionally logs data.

Parameters:

  • voltages (ndarray) – 3-phase input voltages [v_a, v_b, v_c] [V]

  • log_data (bool) – Whether to log data to history, defaults to True

Return type:

None

Warning

Set log_data=False during high-frequency real-time control loops, then call log_data() periodically to reduce overhead.

configure_power_factor_control(enabled, target_pf=0.95, kp=0.1, ki=1.0, max_compensation_var=10000.0, window_samples=128)[source]

Enable/disable closed-loop PFC telemetry hook.

Return type:

None

get_power_factor_control_state()[source]

Return current PFC telemetry and controller state.

Return type:

dict[str, float | bool]

get_efficiency_state()[source]

Return current efficiency and loss telemetry.

Return type:

dict[str, float]

configure_hardware_interface(enabled)[source]

Enable or disable hardware I/O execution path at runtime.

Return type:

None

get_hardware_state()[source]

Return hardware backend execution status and diagnostics.

Return type:

dict[str, Any]

log_data(load_torque=None)[source]

Manual data logging (for external control loops).

Call this periodically when using step() with log_data=False.

Parameters:

load_torque (float | None) – Load torque to log. If None, uses current load profile value.

Return type:

None

reset(initial_speed=0.0)[source]

Reset simulation to initial state.

Clears all history and resets motor and load profile.

Parameters:

initial_speed (float) – Initial motor speed [rad/s], defaults to 0.0

Return type:

None

get_history()[source]

Get all simulation history as numpy arrays.

Returns:

Dictionary with history data arrays

Return type:

dict[str, ndarray]

Keys:

  • ‘time’: Simulation time points [s]

  • ‘currents_a’: Phase A currents [A]

  • ‘currents_b’: Phase B currents [A]

  • ‘currents_c’: Phase C currents [A]

  • ‘omega’: Angular velocity [rad/s]

  • ‘theta’: Angular position [rad]

  • ‘speed’: Speed [RPM]

  • ‘emf_a’: Back-EMF phase A [V]

  • ‘emf_b’: Back-EMF phase B [V]

  • ‘emf_c’: Back-EMF phase C [V]

  • ‘torque’: Electromagnetic torque [N*m]

  • ‘load_torque’: Load torque [N*m]

  • ‘voltages_a’: Applied voltage phase A [V]

  • ‘voltages_b’: Applied voltage phase B [V]

  • ‘voltages_c’: Applied voltage phase C [V]

get_current_state()[source]

Get current motor state.

Returns:

Current motor state variables

Return type:

dict[str, float]

get_controller_phase_currents()[source]

Return phase currents as seen by the control algorithm.

Return type:

ndarray

get_simulation_info()[source]

Get simulation metadata.

Returns:

Simulation information

Return type:

dict[str, Any]

Keys:

  • ‘time’: Current simulation time [s]

  • ‘step_count’: Number of steps executed

  • ‘dt’: Time step [s]

  • ‘history_size’: Number of logged data points

Load Model Module

This module provides various load models for the motor simulation. The load models generate torque profiles that can be constant, ramping, or variable over time.

Supported load types: - Constant load (fixed torque) - Ramp load (linear change in torque over time) - Variable load (custom time-dependent profile)

author:

Amine Khettat

version:

0.8.0

Added in version 0.8.0: Initial implementation of load models

class src.core.load_model.LoadProfile[source]

Bases: ABC

Abstract base class for load profiles.

All load models inherit from this class and implement the get_torque() method to provide time-dependent load torque.

abstractmethod get_torque(time)[source]

Get load torque at specified time.

Parameters:

time (float) – Simulation time [seconds]

Returns:

Load torque [N*m]

Return type:

float

reset()[source]

Reset load profile to initial state.

Not all profiles need resetting, but this method provides a consistent interface.

Return type:

None

class src.core.load_model.ConstantLoad(torque=0.0)[source]

Bases: LoadProfile

Constant load model.

Applies a fixed torque throughout simulation.

Example:
>>> load = ConstantLoad(torque=2.5)
>>> load.get_torque(0.0)
2.5
>>> load.get_torque(10.0)
2.5
__init__(torque=0.0)[source]

Initialize constant load.

Parameters:

torque (float) – Constant load torque [N*m], defaults to 0.0

get_torque(time)[source]

Get constant load torque.

Parameters:

time (float) – Simulation time [seconds] (not used)

Returns:

Load torque [N*m]

Return type:

float

class src.core.load_model.RampLoad(initial=0.0, final=1.0, duration=1.0)[source]

Bases: LoadProfile

Ramping load model.

The load torque ramps linearly from initial value to final value over a specified duration.

Load Profile:

\[\tau(t) = \tau_0 + \frac{(\tau_f - \tau_0)}{t_{ramp}} \cdot t, \quad 0 \leq t \leq t_{ramp}\]
\[\tau(t) = \tau_f \quad (t > t_{ramp})\]
Example:
>>> load = RampLoad(initial=0.0, final=5.0, duration=2.0)
>>> load.get_torque(1.0)  # At t=1s
2.5
>>> load.get_torque(3.0)  # After ramp
5.0
__init__(initial=0.0, final=1.0, duration=1.0)[source]

Initialize ramp load.

Parameters:

  • initial (float) – Initial load torque [N*m], defaults to 0.0

  • final (float) – Final load torque [N*m], defaults to 1.0

  • duration (float) – Ramp duration [seconds], defaults to 1.0

Raises:

ValueError – If duration <= 0

get_torque(time)[source]

Get ramping load torque.

Parameters:

time (float) – Simulation time [seconds]

Returns:

Load torque [N*m]

Return type:

float

class src.core.load_model.VariableLoad(torque_func=None, time_points=None, torque_points=None)[source]

Bases: LoadProfile

Variable load model with custom time-dependent profile.

Allows definition of arbitrary load torque profiles using: - Time-torque paired data points (interpolated) - Custom callback function

Example (with function):
>>> def load_func(t):
...     return 2.0 * np.sin(2*np.pi*t)
>>> load = VariableLoad(torque_func=load_func)
>>> load.get_torque(0.25)
2.0
Example (with time points):
>>> times = [0.0, 1.0, 2.0]
>>> torques = [0.0, 5.0, 2.0]
>>> load = VariableLoad(time_points=times, torque_points=torques)
__init__(torque_func=None, time_points=None, torque_points=None)[source]

Initialize variable load with either function or data points.

Parameters:

  • torque_func (Callable[[float], float] | None) – Custom torque function: f(time) -> torque, optional

  • time_points (list[float] | None) – List of time points [seconds], optional

  • torque_points (list[float] | None) – List of torque values [N*m], optional

Raises:

  • ValueError – If both torque_func and time_points are None

  • ValueError – If time_points and torque_points lengths don’t match

  • ValueError – If time_points not sorted in ascending order

get_torque(time)[source]

Get variable load torque.

Uses linear interpolation if data points are defined, otherwise calls the torque function.

Parameters:

time (float) – Simulation time [seconds]

Returns:

Load torque [N*m]

Return type:

float

Note

For time values beyond the defined range with data points, the value is clamped to the last defined point.

class src.core.load_model.CyclicLoad(offset=0.0, amplitude=1.0, frequency=1.0, phase=0.0)[source]

Bases: LoadProfile

Cyclic load model with sinusoidal profile.

Generates periodic load torque: τ(t) = τ_offset + τ_amplitude * sin(2π*f*t + φ)

Load Profile:

\[\tau(t) = \tau_{offset} + \tau_{amplitude} \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \varphi)\]
Example:
>>> load = CyclicLoad(offset=1.0, amplitude=2.0, frequency=1.0)
>>> load.get_torque(0.0)
1.0
>>> load.get_torque(0.25)  # At peak
3.0
__init__(offset=0.0, amplitude=1.0, frequency=1.0, phase=0.0)[source]

Initialize cyclic load.

Parameters:

  • offset (float) – Offset torque (mean value) [N*m], defaults to 0.0

  • amplitude (float) – Amplitude of oscillation [N*m], defaults to 1.0

  • frequency (float) – Oscillation frequency [Hz], defaults to 1.0

  • phase (float) – Initial phase [radians], defaults to 0.0

Raises:

ValueError – If frequency < 0

get_torque(time)[source]

Get cyclic load torque.

Parameters:

time (float) – Simulation time [seconds]

Returns:

Load torque [N*m]

Return type:

float
